question: what does this video say  for capital gains tax, is it being grandfathered or every now under new rules, does that mean if I sell before 30 June 2027 I can still access 50% discount

runs.name: raw_single
runs.answer: The video explains that existing properties purchased before the budget night (12 May 2026) are not fully grandfathered for capital gains tax (CGT). For CGT, the 50% discount is abolished and replaced with indexation, but there is a transition: you obtain a valuation as of 30 June 2027 which locks in the 50% discount on gains up to that date. If you sell before 30 June 2027, you can still access the 50% discount because the valuation date and new indexation rules take effect from 1 July 2027. The transcript states: 'once you get your valuation on the 30th of June or the 1st of July that will have the 50%' and 'your capital gains tax is going to be locked in from July of next year'.

runs.name: rag_single
runs.answer: The video states that capital gains tax (CGT) is NOT grandfathered for existing properties; only negative gearing is grandfathered. For CGT, existing properties will get a valuation on 30 June or 1 July 2027 that locks in the 50% discount up to that point, after which indexation applies. The transcript does not explicitly address selling before 30 June 2027, but it implies that the 50% discount applies to gains up to the valuation date. If you sell before that valuation, the transcript does not directly confirm full access to the 50% discount, so there is not enough information to definitively answer that part.

runs.name: rag_all
runs.answer: Based on the provided transcript chunks, the capital gains tax discount reform takes effect from **1 July 2027**. If you sell an asset **before 30 June 2027**, you can still access the full 50% discount [4]. The reform is **not fully grandfathered**; instead, it provides transitional protection: gains accrued up to 1 July 2027 retain the old 50% discount, while gains after that date are subject to a new inflation-indexed system with a minimum 30% tax rate [4][8]. For assets bought before 1 July 2027 and sold after, a split calculation applies using a valuation as of that date [4][8]. One video explicitly states “no for CGT” when asked whether existing properties are grandfathered for capital gains tax [9].

subtopics.topic: Mechanics of two-calculation system for assets held across 1 July 2027
subtopics.rationale: The chunks note that for assets held both before and after the transition date, taxpayers must compute gains under the old 50% discount for the pre-30 June 2027 portion and under the new inflation-indexed method for the post-1 July 2027 portion. This dual calculation is described as an 'administrative nightmare' but the exact formula is not detailed. A focused retrieval could clarify the proposed ATO formula or guidance.
subtopics.followup_query: How do you calculate capital gains for assets held both before and after 30 June 2027 under the proposed grandfathering rules?

subtopics.topic: 30% minimum tax rate and its interaction with grandfathered gains
subtopics.rationale: The chunks mention a minimum 30% tax rate on capital gains under the new rules, but do not specify whether this minimum applies to grandfathered gains or only to gains calculated under the inflation-indexed model. The exemptions listed (pensioners, JobSeeker) are also not fully explained. This creates ambiguity that a follow-up query could resolve.
subtopics.followup_query: Does the proposed 30% minimum tax rate on capital gains apply to grandfathered gains from before 1 July 2027, and what are the exemptions?

runs.name: rag_recursive
runs.answer:
## Preserved Answer
Yes, the videos indicate that there is transitional grandfathering. If you sell before 30 June 2027 (i.e., before 1 July 2027), you can still access the full 50% capital gains tax discount [4]. For properties held across the cutoff, gains up to 30 June 2027 are grandfathered under the old 50% discount, while gains after that date are subject to the new rules [4][8]. There is no permanent grandfathering; instead a split calculation applies [4].

## Split Calculation for Properties Held Across the July 2027 Cutoff
For properties bought before the May 12 budget night (or similar reference date) and sold after 1 July 2027, the capital gain is split into two periods. The gain accrued up to 1 July 2027 is calculated using the existing 50% CGT discount method, while the gain after that date is taxed under the new indexation method with a minimum 30% tax rate [s1.1][s1.2]. A registered valuer must determine the property’s market value as at 1 July 2027 to establish a new cost base for the second period [s1.1][s1.2]. Under the indexation method, the original cost base is adjusted for inflation using CPI, and the real gain is then taxed at a minimum 30% rate (up to the taxpayer’s marginal rate) [s1.1][s1.3][s1.5]. For long‑term holders, the indexation adjustment may be small if property growth outpaces inflation, making the effective tax rate typically land between 30–35% [s1.6]. The split is fixed regardless of later price movements; the valuation at 1 July 2027 serves as the hard cut‑off [s1.1].

subtopic_answers.topic: Split calculation for properties held across the July 2027 cutoff
subtopic_answers.followup_query: How is capital gains tax calculated for properties bought before July 1 2027 and sold after, using the split between the old 50% discount and the new indexation method?
subtopic_answers.answer: For properties bought before the May 12 budget night (or similar reference date) and sold after 1 July 2027, the capital gain is split into two periods. The gain accrued up to 1 July 2027 is calculated using the existing 50% CGT discount method, while the gain after that date is taxed under the new indexation method with a minimum 30% tax rate [s1.1][s1.2]. A registered valuer must determine the property’s market value as at 1 July 2027 to establish a new cost base for the second period [s1.1][s1.2]. Under the indexation method, the original cost base is adjusted for inflation using CPI, and the real gain is then taxed at a minimum 30% rate (up to the taxpayer’s marginal rate) [s1.1][s1.3][s1.5]. For long‑term holders, the indexation adjustment may be small if property growth outpaces inflation, making the effective tax rate typically land between 30–35% [s1.6]. The split is fixed regardless of later price movements; the valuation at 1 July 2027 serves as the hard cut‑off [s1.1].

recursion.preserved_first_answer: Yes, the videos indicate that there is transitional grandfathering. If you sell before 30 June 2027 (i.e., before 1 July 2027), you can still access the full 50% discount [4]. For properties held across the cutoff date, gains up to 30 June 2027 are grandfathered under the old 50% discount, while gains after that date are subject to the new rules [4][8]. There is no permanent grandfathering; instead there is a split calculation [4].

runs.answer:
Yes, the 50% capital gains tax discount is being grandfathered. If you sell an asset before 30 June 2027, you can still access the 50% discount because gains accrued up to that date retain the old rules. After 1 July 2027, new rules apply (inflation-indexed model and a minimum 30% tax rate) to new gains [5][9].

## Minimum 30% tax rate on capital gains

Under the new model, a minimum 30% tax rate applies to real capital gains (above inflation), overriding an investor's marginal tax rate. The chunks indicate that this minimum applies regardless of the taxpayer's usual tax bracket—even if retired early and on a 0% or 19% marginal rate, a 30% tax is imposed on the real gains [s1.3]. No exemptions for specific groups (e.g., pensioners, JobSeeker) are discussed in the provided evidence. The rule forces a >30% floor on net real gains, which in practice means a higher effective rate than the old 50% discount for many investors [s1.1][s1.2][s1.4].

## Inflation-indexed capital gains tax model

Under the new model, the 50% discount is replaced by an inflation-adjusted indexation method. The cost base of an asset is adjusted for inflation (using CPI), so only the real gain above inflation is taxable. This real gain is then subject to a minimum 30% tax rate. The model is described as a return to the pre‑1999 approach where only gains exceeding inflation are taxed, but with the addition of a 30% minimum tax floor [s2.1][s2.2]. The impact is sensitive to inflation rates; it introduces complexity around timing of sales and varying effective tax rates (30‑35% estimated range) [s2.1][s2.3].

## Grandfathering mechanics for multi-period holdings

When an asset is bought before 1 July 2027 and sold after that date, two separate calculations are performed. The gain up to the value of the asset on 1 July 2027 is calculated using the old 50% CGT discount method. That valuation must be done by a registered property valuer (not a real estate agent's market appraisal). Any further gain after that date is taxed under the new indexation method (real gain above inflation, with minimum 30% tax). This split‑grandfathering approach means accountants must handle two tax calculations for the same asset [s3.1][s3.2]. The valuation date (1 July 2027) is critical, and investors may be motivated to increase the asset's value before that date to maximise the portion benefiting from the 50% discount [s3.5]. The rules apply across all asset classes, including shares and business assets [s3.3][s3.4].

subtopic_answers.topic: Minimum 30% tax rate on capital gains
subtopic_answers.followup_query: What is the proposed minimum 30% tax rate on capital gains and who is exempt from it?
subtopic_answers.answer: The proposed minimum 30% tax rate applies to real capital gains (above inflation) and overrides an investor's marginal tax rate. The chunks indicate that this minimum applies regardless of the taxpayer's usual tax bracket—even if retired early and on a 0% or 19% marginal rate, a 30% tax is imposed on the real gains [s1.3]. No exemptions for specific groups (e.g., pensioners, JobSeeker) are discussed in the provided evidence. The rule forces a ‘>30% floor’ on net real gains, which in practice means a higher effective rate than the old 50% discount for many investors [s1.1][s1.2][s1.4].

subtopic_answers.followup_query: How does the proposed inflation-indexed capital gains tax model work under the Australian government's reforms?
subtopic_answers.answer: Under the new model, the 50% discount is replaced by an inflation-adjusted indexation method. The cost base of an asset is adjusted for inflation (using CPI), so only the real gain above inflation is taxable. This real gain is then subject to a minimum 30% tax rate. The model is described as a return to the pre‑1999 approach where only gains exceeding inflation are taxed, but with the addition of a 30% minimum tax floor [s2.1][s2.2]. The impact is sensitive to inflation rates; it introduces complexity around timing of sales and varying effective tax rates (30‑35% estimated range) [s2.1][s2.3].

subtopic_answers.followup_query: How is capital gains tax calculated for assets bought before 1 July 2027 and sold after that date under the grandfathering rules?
subtopic_answers.answer: When an asset is bought before 1 July 2027 and sold after that date, two separate calculations are performed. The gain up to the value of the asset on 1 July 2027 is calculated using the old 50% CGT discount method. That valuation must be done by a registered property valuer (not a real estate agent's market appraisal). Any further gain after that date is taxed under the new indexation method (real gain above inflation, with minimum 30% tax). This split‑grandfathering approach means accountants must handle two tax calculations for the same asset [s3.1][s3.2]. The valuation date (1 July 2027) is critical, and investors may be motivated to increase the asset's value before that date to maximise the portion benefiting from the 50% discount [s3.5]. The rules apply across all asset classes, including shares and business assets [s3.3][s3.4].

recursion.preserved_first_answer: Yes, the 50% capital gains tax discount is being grandfathered. If you sell an asset before 30 June 2027, you can still access the 50% discount because gains accrued up to that date retain the old rules. After 1 July 2027, new rules apply (inflation-indexed model and a minimum 30% tax rate) to new gains [5][9].
